Contrast in hydrated specimens should be directly interpretable as structure rather than as stain.
Fixation and drying artifacts are avoided.

Radiation damage is expected to be much more severe in hydrated specimens.

1 sample x 2 conc. x 2 grid types x 3 =
Step 1 : Make 3 x grids for every condition (reproducibility issue)  3 x sample & 3 x time in the lab
Step 2 : Need to Screen all the grids  3 x screening time + data collection
Step 3 : The samples may have issues  back to step 1 & 2 for repeating (multiple times)
1 conc. x 4 grid types x 3 =  12 grids
1 conc. X 1 grid types x 4 additives x 3 = 12 grids
1 conc. X 2 grid types x 3 additives x 2 = 12 grids
1 conc. X 1 grid types x 1 additive = 1 final grid

Visual representation of the distribution of spatial frequencies within an image, obtained by calculating the Fourier transform of the image and then squaring magnitude
Low frequencies (large features, low resolution) appear near the center
High frequencies (small features, high resolution) are towards the edges of the spectrum

Other undesirable effects such as the level of astigmatism, or drift associated with an image, as well as crystalline ice contamination, are often more clearly diagnosed in the power spectrum than in the cryo-TEM image itself
Visual representation of the distribution of spatial frequencies within an image, obtained by calculating the Fourier transform of the image and then squaring magnitude
Low frequencies (large features, low resolution) appear near the center
High frequencies (small features, high resolution) are towards the edges of the spectrum

Other undesirable effects such as the level of astigmatism, or drift associated with an image, as well as crystalline ice contamination, are often more clearly diagnosed in the power spectrum than in the cryo-TEM image itself
The CTF is a measure of how much the phase shift (defocus) and the spatial frequency
However, microscope aberrations (envelop function) have corrupted the image

Oscillations within the CTF are easily seen by visualizing the power spectrum of cryo-EM images. The pattern of concentric rings (Thon rings) represent the alternation of high and low contrast transfer as a function of spatial frequency
Raw cryo-EM data corresponds to a stack of movie frames, which each frame is a sparse image consisting zeros and ones
But particles move due to physical drift and ‘beam-induced motion’
Fractionation into frames (‘movie’) allows correction of drift and motion
The re-aligned frames are then summed into a single, de-blurred micrograph
To optimize the signal-to-noise ratio across all resolutions in the micrograph to enable high-resolution 3D reconstruction
The electron beam damages the sample during the course of exposure
Cryo-EM movie frames accumulate an increasing amount of electron dose.
The early frames – retained high res. info.
The later frames – limited to low res. info.
To optimize the signal-to-noise ratio across all resolutions in the micrograph to enable high-resolution 3D reconstruction

Estimation of CTF parameters to enable the computational correction of the CTF for each image
The most important task in CTF estimation is to determine the true defocus of each image
CTF modeling is also an effective way to assess micrograph.
The cross-correlation and resolution limit of CTF fitting are useful metrics to exclude poor micrographs out of the dataset

ML approach (Topaz, crYOLO, DRPnet, DeepCryoPicker etc.)
ML approach (Topaz, crYOLO, DRPnet, DeepCryoPicker etc.)
Topaz – No general model; requires training per sample/per dataset. Is free standing or incorporated into RELION and cryoSPARC.

Warp – Has a general model but typically requires user re-training with specific sample types. Once trained, the neural network can work across different datasets and similar sample types. (Note: Warp only runs on Windows. Warp also has a junk detector that will automatically avoid junk based on a pre-trained neural network)
crYOLO – Has a general model that works well on most samples. Currently, is free standing or a part of the SPHIRE package.
ML approach (Topaz, crYOLO, DRPnet, DeepCryoPicker etc.)
The Fourier transform of each of the 2D class averages Represent a section of the Fourier transform of the initial object
The inverse TF of the combined sections generates the initial objects

Resolution is calculated using Bragg’s law, which states that the smallest distance between crystal lattice planes that can be resolved (d) is equal to the wavelength of the X-ray (l) devided by twice the sine of the diffraction angle (q)

Resolution is determined by calculating the Fourier Shell Correlation (FSC) between two independently generated ‘half-maps’ of the same data, signifying the point at which the correlation between the maps drops below a set threshold (commonly 0.143) across different resolution shells
Rosenthal Plot (the number of particles vs. resolution)
